pattern: Because merging always produces the full sorted union regardless of order, a subset of the lists has one fixed length and one fixed median no matter how it gets assembled, so the cheapest way to build it can be found once and reused via a bitmask over which lists it contains.
hints: However a group of lists gets merged together, the final sorted values in that group are always the same: just the union of everything in it. So precompute, for every possible subset of the lists, the length and median of its fully merged result.

The cheapest way to merge a subset into one list is to split it into two non-empty parts, pay the merge cost for combining the cheapest way to build each part, and try every way of splitting the subset in two.

Represent each subset as a bitmask over the lists and build up costs from smaller subsets to larger ones (a subset with only one list costs nothing to 'merge'); the answer is the cheapest cost for the subset containing every list.

constraints.note: each lists[i] has length 1..500, is sorted in non-decreasing order, values in -1000000000..1000000000, and the sum of all lists[i].length is at most 2000

edge_inputs.description: the reference example merging three lists in two stages
the reference example with lists of very different lengths
the reference example with the two smallest possible single-element lists
the reference example with identical single-element lists
the minimum of two lists, each with a single element at opposite ends of the value range
the maximum count of twelve lists, each a single element, forcing every possible merge order to be considered
one list is far larger than the others, so naively merging it last is not necessarily optimal
several lists share the exact same median, so merge order among them should not matter
all values identical across every list

stress.description: several small random lists, exercising every subset split
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 12))
    lists = []
    for _ in range(n):
        length = rng.randint(1, 15)
        vals = sorted(rng.randint(-1000, 1000) for _ in range(length))
        lists.append(vals)
    return [lists]

stress.description: the maximum of twelve lists with moderate lengths summing well under the total element budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = 12
    lists = []
    for _ in range(n):
        length = rng.randint(5, 40)
        vals = sorted(rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000) for _ in range(length))
        lists.append(vals)
    return [lists]

stress.description: two lists near the maximum single-list length, stressing the merge step itself
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    length = max(1, min(size, 480))
    a = sorted(rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(length))
    b = sorted(rng.randint(-1000000000, 1000000000) for _ in range(length))
    return [[a, b]]
